Sentence: You step down the narrow terrace, boots finding the patched concrete by habit.
Salt and diesel cling to the air beneath the morning heat, an acrid tang that presses behind your teeth.
Your hand drifts to
the salvaged brass compass at your chest—not because it points any truer now, but because its weight steadies the quick ache behind your ribs.
sand mixed with scoured shell in the creases where doors meet thresholds.
Someone has shoved a flattened tarp under a door and tied a
cord.
Across the row, a bird's wing catches a laundry line and the cloth snaps like a small flag.
You run a mental checklist as you walk.
Sandbags—repaired.
Diversion channels—cleared.
Rooftop rain barrels—topped.
The motions are automatic because they have to be.
Your auburn braid, threaded with shell and seed beads, brushes the collar of
your worn olive jacket; the elbow patch on the left sleeve is a pale crescent of mended hope.
You taste salt.
You blink it away.
You were an engineer once who wore crisp lab coats and presented models in blue-lit auditoriums.
You came home when the grants ran thin and your family’s roofs started to sag.
You came back because the

plans the city signed off on two administrations ago never matched the shoreline you remembered—because your childhood friends raise seedlings on tarps and your brother sprays stencils of tiny hands across concrete.
You keep telling yourself
the work is the same; only the audience is different.
Rafi is already at the nursery rooftop when you climb the ladder.
The greenhouse domes glow with held heat; the scent of wet soil and cut basil folds into the diesel.
He’s crouched over a tray of saplings, gloved hands patient and deliberate.
Morning's heavy.
Tide came in farther than last week's, but the barrels held.
Lio patched one of the eastern levee pockets.
He's got your paint all over his hoodie again.
You kneel beside him, palms caked with compost.
Your fingers like the feel of the soil remind you of why this place still matters.
Did you get electricity from the panels for the pump last night?
Rafi (shrugs) Barely.
Solar's decent when it's calm, but the clouds came in.
I ran the hand pump at midnight.
Mrs.
Ortega says she'd rather help than leave.
You twist the rim of a seedling pot between your fingers, slow.
The rooftop hums with small life—bees, a rooftop cat that watches with baleful interest, the soft ticking of a makeshift water gauge.
• Run my thumb along the compass, pretending it still points
You trace the compass rim.
It doesn't move, but the ritual calms the tightness at your throat.
• Let the braid fall free, tuck it behind your ear and focus on the seedlings
You tuck the braid back.
Dirt clings to your fingertips; the saplings look sharper somehow, immediate and salvageable.
The narrative continues as the rooftop conversation carries on.

You feel that thin low-burning—impatience masked as a steady ache.

Less time for alternatives.

small hands reaching for a tide line.

Morning.
I ran the models overnight with the new boundary conditions.
Some of the worst-case projections are—' (she exhales, searching for words) '—not good.
But there are multiple feasible scenarios that could reduce displacement if the construction is rerouted.
blue gradients that mark likely inundation, green streaks of surviving marsh, red patches where homes would go.
Translate, Sima.
if the wall goes in as designed, expect more concentrated wave energy to the south.

If we include corridors—spaces that allow water to funnel and wetlands to breathe—the system dissipates energy better.

The ticking fan sounds louder for a beat.
You press your palm to the table to steady yourself.
You are impatient—always impatient—because the work needs to move faster than the machinery of politics allows.

intensity is low; there's a slow, careful sorrow under everything, like a tide pool holding a reflected sky.

It moves with bureaucratic indifference while a boy across the street chases a stray dog through ankle-high water.
You feel the pressure increase, subtle as a rising tide—still a slow swell, but inexorable.
They'll send their team.

Lio's future, Mrs.

Your sea-glass eyes
sting—not from wind, but from the chronic low ache of knowing how little time neighborhoods often get.
who will speak where, who will take notes, who will photograph the marsh at low tide.

You catch Lio's eye on your way out.
He gives you a brief, wry nod—half challenge, half plea.
Don't let them make this a done deal.
Not before we speak.
I won't let them.
You step outside into the heat and salty air, the city opening before you like a problem with too many possible fixes and not enough hands.
The diesel and the distant machinery hum again.
The municipal
bulletin on the plaza screen flickers.
For a long second you simply stand and breathe, counting the small, deliberate steps you will have to take.
You feel a low tightening, like the moment before rain when the air holds its breath.
It's not panic.
It's not frantic energy.
It's the steady, grim recognition that the next moves are limited and consequential—and that you are already committed to making them.
You glance at your compass one last time, fingers curling around the tarnished brass.
The needle does not move.
You tuck it back beneath your jacket, braid brushing your palm.
You step into the municipal hall still carrying the heat of the Low Row on your skin, the salt clinging to the cuffs of your jacket.
The place is cool in a way that feels intentional
— air conditioning, cleaned edges, a kind of institutional detachment that smooths the city’s rougher sounds into manageable data points.
not panic, but a slow, corrosive worry that eats at the edges of confidence.
You imagine the marsh in diagram lines, the community garden roofs, Lio’s paint-splattered hoodie on a future that might be half-erased.
Good morning.
Thank you for coming early.
We want this to be efficient.
Efficient at what cost?
At protecting the city.
At moving funds that keep people dry.
We are trying to balance immediacy and feasibility.
You study her scarf, the way she tucks it against her neck, as if warmth and optics can be the same thing.
The atrium smells faintly of coffee and lemon polish.
It feels like standing under a glass dome when outside the city is being rewritten in saltwater.
